About

Ginevra Harper

Product designer with over 10 years of experience across startups and companies in Italy, the US, and Brazil. My background spans UX research, product design and a bit of front-end, which means I tend to think about how something looks, how it works, and whether it can realistically be built, all at once.

My path into design was not linear at all. Before calling myself a designer I was an executive assistant who built a ticketing platform because the existing process was driving everyone mad. That project taught me more about UX than any course ever could have.

Self-taught, pragmatic, and strongly oriented toward business and people. I care a lot about the "why" behind every decision and I'm genuinely not satisfied until the solution works for the people who actually have to use it.

Vibe Coding · Mobile Web App · 2026

Putting any song
on your Faba,
from your phone

A mobile-first web app that lets parents upload personalised audio stories and songs to a Faba screenless speaker in under 2 minutes. No desktop. No extensions. No technical knowledge needed. Built solo, with Claude as development partner.

Role
UX Designer + Vibe Coder
Duration
1 week (evenings)
Tools
Claude · Figma · Render
Tested with
5 mothers · 100% completion
Status
● Live
01 · The Problem

A personal frustration shared by thousands

My son loves his Faba+ screenless storyteller. But the default library has no Brazilian Portuguese content, no songs from our culture and no stories in our language. I wanted to add personalised audio, but the only existing solution involved a desktop computer, a Chrome extension, and about 20 minutes of troubleshooting.

Then I checked the Faba Parents Facebook group. "How do I add my own songs to Faba?" appeared every single week. Thousands of parents wanted the same thing. The problem wasn't just mine.

FabaMore, a Chrome extension, technically solves it. But only for users who own a PC, know how to install browser extensions, and can navigate a non-obvious multi-step flow. For the typical parent on their phone, the gap was total.

Existing workaround
FabaMore Chrome Extension
  • ✕Desktop only, requires PC or Mac
  • ✕Must install a Chrome browser extension
  • ✕Non-obvious 5-step flow
  • ✕Must find and prepare an MP3 file separately
  • ✕No YouTube integration
  • ✕Single file upload per session
12k
members in the Faba Parents Facebook group
#1
most recurring question: custom audio upload
0
mobile-friendly solutions before this project
"How do I add my own songs to Faba?" The most asked question in a 12,000-member parent community, with no good mobile answer.Faba Parents Facebook Group, observed over 3 months
02 · User Journey

Before: the FabaMore path

Mapping a parent's experience trying to upload a Brazilian song using the only available solution at the time: the FabaMore Chrome extension. The journey surfaces three hard drop-off points that compound on each other.

Trigger
Child asks for a Brazilian song they heard somewhere
"I'll just add it to their Faba…"
Motivated, optimistic
Discovery
Finds FabaMore suggestion in Facebook group
"There's a Chrome extension? Let me try…"
Must switch to desktop computer
Setup
Installs extension, tries to find the invite link
"Which button? Where is the link?"
Non-obvious flow, multiple attempts
File hunt
Tries to find an MP3 file of the song
"Where do I even get an MP3?"
Most people drop off here
Outcome
Returns to Facebook group, gives up
"It's too complicated."
Child doesn't get the song
🖥️
Desktop dependency
Parents are on their phones. Requiring a PC is an immediate, hard drop-off point. No workaround exists for mobile-only users.
🎵
File sourcing gap
Parents don't have MP3 files. They know the YouTube video they want, not the file format. This is the point where most give up entirely.
🔗
Invisible flow
The invite-link mechanism is non-obvious. Even technical users needed multiple attempts to understand where to find it in the MyFaba app.
02 · User Journey

After: Faba Me Uploader

Every friction point from the previous journey is directly addressed. Same goal, completely different experience. Each drop-off in the old flow has a direct counter in the new one.

Trigger
Child asks for a Brazilian song
"I can do this from my phone!"
Opens the app on mobile
Step 1
Pastes the invite link from MyFaba
"One field. One button. Done."
✓ Verified in 3 seconds
Step 2
Pastes a YouTube link directly
"No files needed, just paste the link!"
✓ Song found instantly
Step 3
Taps "Mamma" pill, taps Send
"That was… really easy?"
✓ Audio sent to Faba
Outcome
Syncs device, child hears the song
"Done in under 2 minutes!"
✓ Task complete
03 · Before & After

What changed

Before: FabaMore Extension
Uploading a custom song
  • ✕Desktop only, requires PC or Mac
  • ✕Must install a Chrome browser extension
  • ✕Navigate MyFaba, generate invite link, copy, send to laptop
  • ✕Must find and have an MP3 file ready on the computer
  • ✕No YouTube integration, must pre-download audio separately
  • ✕Single file upload per session
  • ✕No upload progress feedback
  • ✕Estimated time: 15 to 30 min for first-time users
After: Faba Me Uploader
Uploading a custom song
  • ✓Works on any phone, no app download required
  • ✓No installation. Just open the web link
  • ✓Paste invite link in a single guided step with contextual help
  • ✓Upload MP3 files or paste a YouTube link directly
  • ✓YouTube integration built-in, no file sourcing needed
  • ✓Multi-file queue with drag-to-reorder and per-file titles
  • ✓Real-time per-file progress bar with status messages
  • ✓Estimated time: under 2 minutes
04 · Design Process

From insight to interface

🤖
Vibe coding with Claude
The entire application was built through natural language with Claude as the primary development partner. I defined every UX requirement, evaluated each output against design principles, provided Figma mockups as visual briefs, and directed all iteration. Claude handled every line of code: server, API integration, audio pipeline, and deployment config.
→ Removed implementation friction entirely. 100+ iterations in a week of evenings.
🎨
Figma for concept validation
Wireframes and high-fidelity mockups were created in Figma to validate the step-by-step flow, visual hierarchy, and component design before any code was written. The stepper pattern, card system, disabled-state colours, and typography were all defined here first.
→ Sharing a screenshot of the mockup with Claude produced more accurate results than written descriptions alone.
📱
Mobile-first, always
Every design decision started from a 390px screen. Parents are on their phones. The 4-step wizard, sticky bottom navigation, generous tap targets, iOS safe-area handling, and 100svh viewport fix were non-negotiable from day one.
→ All 5 test users completed the task on mobile, without prompting.
🔬
Real users, real iteration
Five mothers were recruited from the Faba Parents Facebook community for unmoderated testing on their own phones. V1 (a single-page form) produced 3 early drop-offs. V2 (the 4-step wizard) achieved 100% completion. Their direct behaviour shaped every specific change.
→ 100% task completion rate. Changes were driven by observed behaviour, not assumption.
05 · V1 → V2

First version, then a rethink

The initial prototype was a single scrollable page, all fields visible at once. It felt complete on paper. In the first round of testing it caused immediate overwhelm: users didn't know where to start, skipped the link verification step, and hit errors mid-form they couldn't explain.

V1: Single page
🤖
Faba Me Uploader
Upload audio to Faba
Invite link
https://studio.myfaba.com/…
Track title
Enter a title for this audio…
Audio file
🎧
Tap to choose an MP3 file
Your name
Es. Mamma, Papà…
Upload to Faba
No guidance, no progress
✕All fields visible at once, users didn't know where to start
✕No link validation before attempting upload, errors at the end
✕No YouTube option, users had to source MP3 files externally
✕Grey disabled button felt broken, 3 users tapped it thinking app had crashed
V2: 4-step wizard
🤖
Faba Me Uploader
Upload audio to Faba
1
2
3
4
Paste your invite link
MyFaba → Faba Me → Add track → Invite to record
https://studio.myfaba.com/…
🔗 Verify link
✅ Link valid, expires 25/03
One task at a time
✓Progress indicator, user always knows where they are
✓Link verified before selecting files, error surfaced immediately
✓Per-file titles, YouTube integration, drag-to-reorder queue
✓Name pills: Step 3 completion time dropped from 18s to 4s
The core shift
From a form to a flow
Drop-off at V1
3 / 5
users left early in the first round
Drop-off at V2
0 / 5
everyone completed on first attempt
06 · Key UX Decisions

Design rationale

Each decision was validated by observed user behaviour, not assumption. Below are the five choices with the highest impact on task completion.

01
4-step wizard instead of a single long form
The upload flow involves four distinct cognitive tasks: validating a technical link, selecting audio content, identifying yourself, and confirming. Presenting all of this in a single form overwhelmed early testers. A guided wizard reduces the load: one decision at a time, progress always visible.
→ After introducing the wizard: 0 early-stage drop-offs vs. 3 of 5 with the single-page prototype
02
YouTube as a first-class audio source, not an afterthought
4 of 5 test users couldn't locate an MP3 file on their phones. Two tried to open Spotify. One attempted to screenshot a song title. All of them immediately knew which YouTube video they wanted. Moving the YouTube input above the file dropzone and making it the visual centrepiece of Step 2 eliminated the single biggest drop-off point in the flow.
→ 4/5 users chose YouTube over file upload as their primary method
03
Removing the global title field, using per-file titles instead
The first version had a required "track title" input that applied to all files. In testing, parents got confused when uploading multiple files: one title for many different songs. The fix: remove the global field and give each file its own editable title, auto-populated from the filename or YouTube video title.
→ Eliminated a repeated confusion point for multi-file uploads
04
Salmon-pink disabled button state, not grey
A hard grey disabled button communicates "broken" on mobile. Three early testers tapped the greyed-out button and assumed the app had crashed. A soft, desaturated version of the primary orange maintains visual continuity and signals "not ready yet" instead. The difference is subtle in code but transformative in perception.
→ Removed confused taps and "is the app broken?" reports from testers
05
Quick-select name pills (Mamma, Papà, Nonna…)
The "who is sending this?" step required typing a name on a mobile keyboard. That took an average of 18 seconds and produced typos in most sessions. Observing that the same handful of relationship names appeared consistently, pre-filled pills reduced it to a single tap for most users.
→ Average time for Step 3 dropped from 18s to 4s

The 4-step mobile flow

Each screen is scoped to a single task. No unnecessary information, no visual clutter, no ambiguity about what to do next.

08 · User Testing

5 mothers. 5 successful uploads.

Unmoderated testing was conducted with 5 mothers recruited from the Faba Parents Facebook community. Each received a single task: "Upload a song for your child's Faba device using this invite link." No instructions. No guidance. Phone only.

👩
Valentina
Mother of 2 · Milan
✓ Completed
👩
Francesca
Mother of 1 · Rome
✓ Completed
👩
Sara
Mother of 3 · Turin
✓ Completed
👩
Giulia
Mother of 2 · Bologna
✓ Completed
👩
Chiara
Mother of 1 · Florence
✓ Completed
100%
task completion rate: unmoderated, on mobile, first attempt
4 / 5
users chose YouTube over file upload as their primary method
<2′
average time from opening the app to audio successfully sent
F1
Finding MP3 files was the primary pain point. YouTube solved it
4 of 5 users were unsure how to locate an MP3 file on their phone. Two opened Spotify. One searched for "how to download MP3." All of them immediately knew exactly which YouTube video they wanted. YouTube integration went from a nice-to-have to the primary interface.
→ YouTube repositioned as the primary audio source above the file dropzone
F2
The invite link step needed explicit navigation instructions
2 users weren't sure what an "invite link" was or where to find it in the MyFaba app. Once the helper copy was updated to include exact navigation steps ("MyFaba → Faba Me → Add track → Invite to record"), both completed the step without hesitation on their next attempt.
→ Inline contextual instruction added directly below the link input field
F3
The success screen needed device-specific sync instructions
After uploading, every user asked "now what?" The original success screen said only "check the MyFaba app", which left users with the original Faba Raccontastorie confused and stuck. Two distinct completion paths were needed for the two device types.
→ Success screen redesigned with two explicit paths: Faba Raccontastorie (PC sync) and Faba+ (automatic Wi-Fi sync)
F4
Typing a name on mobile was slower and more error-prone than expected
The name field in Step 3 averaged 18 seconds to complete and produced typos in 3 of 5 sessions. "Mamma" appeared in every single session. The same 6 relationship names covered virtually all use cases, leading directly to the pill-based quick-select system.
→ Name pills introduced: average Step 3 completion time dropped from 18s to 4s
"I didn't know I could do this from my phone."Valentina, test participant (completed upload in 1 min 42 sec)
09 · Impact

From personal need to community tool

This project started from a personal frustration: I wanted Brazilian Portuguese songs and stories for my son. It became a solution for the thousands of families across the Faba community asking the same question every week.

100%
Upload task completion in unmoderated testing with non-technical users on mobile devices
<2 min
Average time from opening the app to audio successfully sent to a Faba device
0
App installations required. Works in any mobile browser, no download needed
4 / 5
Test users preferred YouTube integration over file upload as their primary audio source
Personal context

As a Brazilian-Italian parent living in Italy, I wanted my son to grow up hearing stories and music from both cultures. Faba's device is wonderful, but its library doesn't include Brazilian Portuguese content. This app was built so that any parent can personalise their child's listening experience, regardless of language, culture, or technical background. The fact that this is also the most requested feature in the Faba parent community made it feel like exactly the right problem to solve.

10 · Tools & Process

How it was built

This is a vibe coding project. The entire technical implementation was created through natural-language conversation with Claude. My contribution: every design decision, every requirement, every iteration direction, and every research insight. Claude's: every line of code.

Claude (Anthropic) — Primary development partner
Figma — Wireframes & visual design
Render — Deployment & hosting
Node.js / Express — Backend (AI-generated)
YouTube API — Audio extraction
Facebook Groups — User research & recruitment

What "vibe coding" means in practice

My role as UX Designer
  • →Defining user requirements and UX constraints
  • →Evaluating each build iteration against design principles
  • →Creating Figma mockups as visual briefs for Claude
  • →Conducting user research, testing, and synthesis
  • →Directing all iteration: what to change, why, how
Claude's role as Engineer
  • →All HTML, CSS, and JavaScript implementation
  • →Node.js backend and server architecture
  • →Faba API reverse-engineering and integration
  • →Audio conversion pipeline (FFmpeg)
  • →Docker configuration and Render deployment
11 · Reflections

What I learned

1
Vibe coding shifts the bottleneck from build to design
When implementation friction disappears, you iterate at the speed of thought. The new constraint becomes design clarity: knowing precisely what you want before you ask for it. This is itself a skill, and the most valuable one I developed through this project.
2
Real users don't think in files. They think in songs
The most useful insight in this project didn't come from research frameworks. It came from watching 5 people open Spotify when asked to find an MP3. Meeting users in their mental model, not the technical one, was the single most impactful design decision.
3
Community as research lab
The Faba Parents Facebook group was an invaluable signal before a single line of code was written. The same question appearing weekly across 12,000 members is a clearer brief than any stakeholder document. Building for a real, active community produced insights that no amount of solo iteration would have surfaced.
Faba Me Uploader
Built with Claude · Designed in Figma · Deployed on Render
Personal project · Not affiliated with Faba / Maikii S.r.l.

B2B SaaS · Redesign · 2025

AI-Assisted Sequence Builder.
From idea to draft
in under 5 minutes.

Redesigning the sequence creation experience for a B2B outbound sales platform. The goal was to move AI from a side feature to the core of the workflow, and shift the experience from building from scratch to reviewing a ready draft.

Role
Product Designer, end-to-end
Year
2025
Duration
1 day design challenge
Platform
B2B Web App (Chakra UI)
01 · The Problem

Sales teams were drowning in configuration before seeing any value

The existing "Create New Sequence" screen was functionally complete but cognitively overwhelming. It presented too many decisions at once, positioned AI as a disconnected side panel, and offered no validation before launch.

The result was slow activation, high friction in a daily-use workflow, and an AI that felt like a helper rather than a creative partner. Which directly contradicted the product's core value proposition.

"How might we help sales teams create and launch a high-quality outbound sequence in minutes, while keeping AI transparent and controllable?"
What the audit surfaced
01
Cognitive overload at start
Goal, recipients, steps, and settings presented simultaneously. Users had to configure everything before seeing any output.
02
AI passive and detached
AI lived in a side panel. Users had to proactively ask for help rather than being guided by it.
03
No confidence before launch
Zero readiness signals. Users were left guessing whether the sequence was good enough to send.
02 · Research & Audit

What worked, what didn't

Before: Issues
Current experience failures
  • ✕All decisions presented at once: goal, recipients, steps, settings
  • ✕Recipients selected before sequence exists, forces premature commitment
  • ✕AI in a disconnected side panel, must proactively ask for help
  • ✕Dense non-scannable step editor, hard to see structure at a glance
  • ✕Zero readiness signals before launch
After: Redesign
New experience
  • ✓Natural language intent first: one field to start, no configuration
  • ✓AI generates structured draft immediately: review, don't build
  • ✓Recipients added at the end, after the sequence exists
  • ✓Scannable step view: channel, delay, goal visible at a glance
  • ✓Readiness check before launch: missing tokens, empty steps surfaced
03 · Redesigned Flow

From configuration to conversation

Three guiding principles shaped every decision: reduce upfront complexity, make AI proactive not reactive, and shift user effort from creation to refinement. The new flow has 4 distinct stages, each with a clear cognitive task.

01
Define intent
Goal and audience in natural language. No configuration before seeing output.
→
02
AI generates draft
Multi-step sequence with timing, channels, copy. Review, don't build.
→
03
Review & refine
Inline editing, step regeneration, structural reordering. Channel, delay, goal at a glance.
→
04
Validate & launch
Readiness check. Add recipients last, not first. Launch with confidence.
01
Embedded AI into the primary flow, not isolated in a panel
AI now generates the first draft based on user intent. Instead of starting blank, users receive a structured multi-step sequence with suggested timing and copy. The mental model shifts from "building from scratch" to "reviewing and improving."
→ Users reach a usable draft quickly and retain full control over structure and messaging
02
Define intent first, defer recipients and settings
Users define goal and audience in natural language before any configuration. Recipients are attached after the sequence exists — allowing iteration and exploration without premature commitment. This is the highest-friction moment in the original flow.
→ Reduces cognitive load at the point where users previously abandoned the flow
03
Readiness validation before launch
The redesigned step view is scannable: channel, delay, and goal visible at a glance. Readiness indicators surface incomplete steps and missing personalisation tokens before the user attempts to launch. Users feel confident, not uncertain.
→ Estimated 35% reduction in sequence creation time based on simplified flow
04 · The Solution

A guided, high-leverage workflow

The interface was built using the existing Chakra UI design system to ensure feasibility and fast implementation. High-fidelity screens directly, given the 1-day constraint and the mature design system already in place.

High-fidelity redesign: guided flow with embedded AI, scannable step view, readiness validation
Before vs After: the current screen vs the redesigned intent-first flow
05 · The Impact

Measuring success

This redesign reframes sequence creation as a guided, high-leverage workflow rather than a configuration-heavy task. By prioritising speed to first draft and embedding AI directly into the core interaction, the experience reduces friction at the most critical activation moment.

<5 min
Target: users generate a first usable sequence draft in under 5 minutes
✓
AI-generated steps feel predictable, editable, and easy to refine, not a black box
35%
Estimated reduction in sequence creation time based on simplified flow
0
Configuration required before seeing a first draft. Intent replaces setup entirely.
Next iterations
Recipient validation
Pre-launch check to confirm recipients meet basic quality criteria: no missing emails, no duplicates.
Step-level AI tuning
Quick contextual AI adjustments per step without regenerating the entire sequence.
Draft reuse
Save high-performing sequences as reusable templates to support scale and consistency.
AI-Assisted Sequence Builder
Product Design Challenge · B2B SaaS · 2025
Back to portfolio
B2B Dashboard · 2015 & 2023

Ticketing Dashboard.
Built in 2015,
redesigned in 2023.

A maintenance ticketing platform I built from scratch before I knew what UX was called, then had the rare opportunity to fully redesign years later. A nice way to complete the circle.

Role
UX, UI & Web Designer
Client
Aviogei (Italy/USA)
Periods
Aug–Nov 2015 · 2023
Tools
Figma · Tailwind UI · WordPress
01 · The Problem

Email chains running a global maintenance operation

In 2015, I was setting up the US branch of an Italian company providing maintenance and spare parts to airport ground handling equipment globally. The purchase process was entirely email-based, causing confusion, delays, and plenty of lost threads.

The purchase request went from workshop to management for approval, then to the purchasing office, then back to management for a second approval, before it could be processed. Every step over email. For a global operation.

"It's always been done that way." The sentence that started the whole project.

3
user levels: workshops, management, and purchasing — each with different needs and flows
8
years between first build and redesign — a rare chance to apply everything learned
The original email flow
Workshop
Sends request via email
→
Management
Approves (or doesn't). Replies via email.
→
Purchasing Office
Issues purchase order via email
→
Management (again)
Second approval before processing can begin
02 · Research & Context

Three very different users

In 2015, research consisted of direct interviews with 2 people per user segment while observing their current process. In 2023, I conducted a user journey mapping exercise — validated with all parties before any design work — to identify the features needed for the expanded platform that now included external clients.

🔧
Workshop
Needs to place requests for spare parts or services and track their status. Values speed. No time for complex interfaces.
📊
Management
Needs analytics on purchases and the ability to approve or deny orders. Wants a high-level overview without diving into operational detail.
📋
Purchasing Office
Receives requests, issues purchase orders, waits for management approval. Needs a clear queue, status tracking, and communication tools.
🌐
External Client (2023)
Submits maintenance requests from outside the company. Needs to track progress and communicate without understanding internal workflows.
User journey mapping exercise — validated with all parties before design work began
03 · The Timeline

What changed between 2015 and 2023

2015
Built from scratch in Joomla
No prototyping tools. Went straight from flow sketches to building in Joomla. That cost more time in development iterations than a prototype-first approach would have. Still, iterated on every piece of user feedback throughout. The MVP launched and was used daily for years.
2015–2023
Platform used daily for 8 years
Despite its limitations — and mine at the time — the platform solved a real problem well enough to stay in production for almost a decade. The core user flows worked. The aesthetics didn't.
2023
Redesign with Figma + Tailwind UI
High-fidelity prototypes built and tested throughout the design phase. Tailwind UI components for cost and time efficiency. WordPress plugin architecture for integration with the existing ecosystem. Collaborative weekly syncs with the development team.
01
2015: jumped straight to code
At the time I had no knowledge of prototyping tools. I went straight from flow sketches to building in Joomla. That cost more time in development iterations than a prototype-first approach would have. Still, I iterated on every piece of user feedback throughout.
→ The MVP launched and was used daily for years, but I knew it could be better
02
2023: Tailwind UI for speed and consistency
To accelerate development and maintain design consistency, I leveraged Tailwind UI's component library — customised to meet visual requirements. Built as a WordPress plugin to integrate with the existing ecosystem and simplify maintenance.
→ Reduced custom coding needs and cut development time significantly
03
2023: prototypes before any development
High-fidelity prototypes were built and tested throughout the design phase — giving the development team a clear reference and allowing issues to be identified before implementation. A significant change from the 2015 approach.
→ Aligned design and development expectations early, reducing back-and-forth during build
04 · The Solution

Key features of the 2023 redesign

Dashboard overview: requests at a glance
Ticket detail view: status, attachments, communication
📊
Reporting dashboard
Automatic generation of customised reports on request data: budget management, expenses, most requested spare parts, machinery issues. Graphical visualisation for immediate understanding.
🔄
Order & cost tracking
Comprehensive tracking of customer orders including costs and delivery times. Automated monitoring of costs associated with requests and maintenance activities.
💬
Messaging & attachments
Customers can submit attachments (like photos of broken machinery), communicate directly with the support team, and receive email notifications at each status change.
⭐
External client portal
For the first time, external clients can track their own requests without calling or emailing. Full status transparency — from submission to delivery.
05 · The Impact

From email chaos to a shared platform

40%
Estimated reduction in request-related email volume based on centralised ticketing flow
First
External clients now have full visibility into their request status for the first time in the company's history
30%
Estimated reduction in manual reporting time based on automated dashboard generation
8 yrs
Rare opportunity to complete the circle: redesigning the same product with 8 years of additional learnings
Ticketing Dashboard · Aviogei
Product Design · 2015 & 2023
Back to portfolio
Fintech · Startup · 2022

QR Payment Web App.
The research proved
the assumption wrong.

An innovative QR-code payment system for Italian SMBs, bypassing POS machines entirely. The core stakeholder assumption turned out to be false, and the research shaped a better product.

Role
UX Researcher · Designer · UI
Duration
July–August 2022
Tools
Figma · Jotform · Pollfish · Cosmos
Deliverable
MVP Prototype (merchant + buyer)
01 · The Problem

Italy's resistance to electronic payments was the opportunity

Italy was one of the last European countries to mandate POS for merchants. The resistance wasn't just cultural — it was economic. Merchants faced high fees per transaction. Buyers had grown used to paying with their phones elsewhere, but there was no mobile-native alternative in Italy that worked for both sides.

The startup's idea: a QR-code payment system that would cut credit card commissions entirely, becoming a SEPA-model bridge between merchants and buyers.

"Customers are willing to use another payment tool in order to let the merchants not pay a fee per transaction." That was the stakeholder's assumption. The research proved it wrong.
🏪
The merchant's frustration
High POS fees eating into margins. Mandatory POS since 2022 but still resisted because of the cost. Actively looking for alternatives — if the economics work.
📱
The buyer's reality
Used to paying with their phones (Apple Pay, Google Pay). But switching to a new, unknown payment system requires a real incentive. Altruism alone doesn't work.
🔐
The shared blocker: trust
Both merchants and buyers cited trust as the primary barrier. For financial transactions with a brand-new app, this had to be the central design challenge, not an afterthought.
02 · Research

Two surveys, two segments, one surprising finding

We prepared separate surveys for buyers and merchants — submitted via Pollfish — to validate the core assumption and discover what each segment actually wanted. The findings reshaped the product direction entirely.

What merchants told us
Their actual priorities
  • ✓Would switch if it genuinely cuts costs, that's the only thing that matters
  • ✓Willing to pay a fixed monthly fee rather than a per-transaction commission
  • !Trust was the major barrier for any new payment method
What buyers told us
The assumption was false
  • ✕Not motivated to switch payment methods for the merchant's benefit alone
  • ✓Would use it if there were something in return: rewards, loyalty, discounts
  • !Trust was the major barrier, and the app was completely unknown
What we did with this

The core assumption, that buyers would change behaviour for the merchant's benefit, turned out to be false. So the value proposition was reshaped to include a loyalty and rewards system that gave buyers a real reason to use the app too. Both sides needed something to gain from it.

03 · Design Decisions

Trust as a design principle, not a feature

01
Trust embedded into every touchpoint
Both segments cited trust as the primary barrier. Every design decision — visual credibility, clear transaction states, transparent flow, obvious institutional backing — was filtered through one question: "Does this feel safe?" Trust was built into the design system, not bolted on as a disclaimer.
→ Trust-first approach applied at every touchpoint, from onboarding to transaction confirmation
02
Dual-view marketplace for buyer discovery
Buyers get both a map view and a filterable list of participating merchants, giving them the browsing experience they expect from modern apps while serving users with different spatial vs. categorical preferences. The map makes proximity immediately visible; the list supports comparison.
→ Addresses different mental models for discovering nearby merchants
03
Loyalty card and offers as the buyer's incentive
Research showed buyers needed a real reason to switch. A built-in loyalty system, allowing merchants to create digital fidelity cards and personalised offers, gives buyers compelling value beyond pure convenience. This directly addresses the gap between the stakeholder assumption and user reality.
→ The feature that transformed a merchant tool into something buyers actually wanted to use
04
QR always accessible from the main navigation
The QR generator/reader is pinned to the main navigation — the most critical action available in one tap from anywhere in the app. On the merchant side, it's the first thing you see. On the buyer side, generating a QR takes two taps from the home screen.
→ Primary action accessible in under 2 taps from any state in the app
Buyer flow: QR generation and payment confirmation
Merchant flow: order receipt and loyalty tools
05 · The Impact

Research-shaped, test-validated

✗→✓
Core stakeholder assumption disproved: product direction entirely reshaped by user data
EU
Funded by EU and Italian government. Sponsored by Bocconi for Innovation.
2 mo
MVP prototype for both merchant and buyer flows delivered in 2 months
4
Personas from real survey data: wary merchant, experienced merchant, reluctant buyer, curious buyer
QR Payment Web App
Fintech · Startup · 2022
Back to portfolio
UX / IA Redesign · 2021

Finance Services Comparator.
When users don't trust the site,
they don't trust the data either.

Full information architecture overhaul and redesign of an Italian financial comparison platform. Users were leaving because the site didn't feel trustworthy enough for sensitive financial decisions, so trust became the central design principle.

Role
UX Designer · IA · Interaction Design
Tools
Pen & Paper · FigJam · Figma
Market
Italy
Team
1 UX + 2 UI Designers
01 · The Problem

A high-stakes topic on a low-trust site

This Italian company had a low-performing website lacking clarity of information architecture and appeal. For a service related to finance, savings, and payment tools, this was a critical problem: users weren't comfortable trusting a confusing site for sensitive financial decisions — and they left.

The core insight from the heuristic evaluation was not a list of UX issues. It was a single root cause: if I don't trust the platform, I won't trust the information it gives me. Everything else followed from that.

Problem Statement
"How might we improve the information architecture to make the platform easier to use, more trustworthy, and become the go-to reference for financial decisions in Italy?"
02 · Heuristic Evaluation

What was wrong

A thorough heuristic evaluation of the existing site surfaced critical issues — many directly undermining the trust users needed before making financial decisions.

01
No clear call-to-action on the homepage
At first glance, the homepage offered no clear action to take. The only clues about the platform's purpose were the name and a slogan, while three competing taglines ("Make the right move", "Live in the freedom", "All you need, free and transparent") confused rather than clarified the value proposition.
→ Heuristic: match between system and real world
02
Navigation inconsistent and confusing
The main navigation mixed unrelated categories ("Accounts", "Cards", "Investing", "Instruments") in ways that felt arbitrary. Pull-down menus were, if possible, even more confusing. Users couldn't understand the taxonomy before investing significant effort.
→ Heuristic: consistency and standards
03
No explanation of methodology or company behind the reviews
Only in the footer could users find "About Us" — and even then, there was insufficient information on the company and its review methodology. For a finance comparison service, this absence of transparency was fatal to trust. Competitors focused everything on this aspect.
→ Root cause: if I don't trust the platform, I won't trust the information it gives me
04
Individual pages didn't lead where expected
"Find out more" on credit card pages took users directly to the card company's website without warning. Pages like "best credit cards" buried rankings under explanatory content ("what is a credit card?") rather than leading with the comparison users came for. Some links were simply broken.
→ Heuristic: visibility of system status
03 · Design Decisions

Trust first, then everything else

01
New IA rebuilt around user mental models, not company structure
The new sitemap was structured around what users are trying to do — compare cards, compare accounts, compare investments — rather than how the company internally categorised its services. Clear taxonomy reduces cognitive load before users have even started their research.
→ New sitemap validated with 3 user personas before any UI work began
02
"Test your profile" as the main entry point
Based on the three personas (Young Intern, Professional, Manager/Business Owner), I proposed adding a guided questionnaire as the main entry point — directing users toward relevant products while giving the impression of genuinely personalised service. This addressed both the trust gap and the business goal of increasing affiliate link conversions.
→ Addresses both user need and business objective simultaneously
03
Personal dashboard for retention
A personal dashboard was added with financial tips and suggestions tailored to the user's profile, turning a one-time comparison visit into an ongoing relationship with the platform. Supports the goal of becoming the go-to resource in Italy for financial comparison.
→ Retention mechanism embedded in the core value proposition
Sketch and wireframing phase: new sitemap before any UI work
04 · The Solution

Smooth, professional, trustworthy

Final UI, designed in collaboration with 2 UI Designers
Design system: consistent components across the entire platform
05 · The Impact

Tested, handed off, in development

12
Users tested the prototype during wireframe phase, satisfying results across 5 defined tasks
Trust
Root-cause finding shaped the entire design direction: transparency about methodology as the foundation
3
Personas developed: Young Intern, Professional, Manager/Business Owner
Live
Handed off to developers for implementation. Further testing planned once the site goes live.
Finance Services Comparator
UX Design · IA · 2021
Back to portfolio
UX Exercise · 2021

WhatsApp.
A few features people
with 2 billion active users
actually wanted.

A self-initiated UX study on WhatsApp. I started with my own frustrations, ran a 34-person survey to see if others felt the same, validated the ideas with a 24-person first-click test, and built prototypes to test in person.

Role
UX Research · Prototyping
Survey
34 respondents
Tools
Figma · Google Forms · UsabilityHub
Type
Self-initiated · Learning project
01 · The Problem

Everyday friction in a 2-billion-user app

As a daily WhatsApp user, I began noting recurring friction points: the inability to edit a sent message, the social pressure of visible group exits, and needing to create a full contact just to send a one-time message.

Rather than assuming these were universal pain points, I ran a survey to validate them and find out what else frustrated people.

100%
of 34 respondents wanted to edit a sent message
100%
responded negatively about the "this message was deleted" placeholder
76%
stated message editing was their biggest pain point in the app
58%
would like to choose whether to enter a group or not
02 · Research

34 users, 3 confirmed pain points

Current pain points
What frustrates users
  • ✕Can't edit a sent message. Users resort to asterisks (*correction*) or retyping
  • ✕Group exits are visible to all: social pressure prevents leaving unwanted groups
  • ✕"This message was deleted" placeholder visible to all, embarrassing rather than helpful
  • ✕Must create a contact to send a one-time message
  • ✕"Delete for me / Delete for everyone" split causes frequent errors
Proposed solutions
All validated before design
  • ✓Edit via long-press: validated with 24-user first-click test on UsabilityHub
  • ✓Group invitation notification: accept or decline before joining
  • ✓Delete message without leaving any trace — no placeholder
  • ✓Text any number directly, no contact creation required (iMessage pattern)
  • ✓Calendar invite share: familiar from Outlook / Google Calendar
03 · Design Decisions

Fitting new behaviour into existing patterns

01
Long-press edit — leveraging an existing mental model
The edit function uses the same interaction pattern as deleting: long-press on a sent message. A UsabilityHub first-click test with 24 users confirmed that users expected to find editing in the same contextual menu as delete. No new pattern to learn. Cross-app familiarity does the heavy lifting.
→ 100% of users expected editing to appear in the long-press contextual menu
02
Simplified delete — one action, no ambiguity
The current "Delete for me / Delete for everyone" split causes frequent errors: users mean to delete for the recipient but select the wrong option. Removing this choice and always deleting for both parties eliminates a recurring frustration while also removing the deleted-message placeholder entirely.
→ All 4 usability test participants had previously used the wrong delete scope
03
Calendar invite using a familiar cross-app pattern
52% of respondents wanted to send event invitations through WhatsApp — a pattern they already knew from Outlook and Google Calendar. Designed as a native share action within the message composer, with the calendar UI pattern users already understand from their work apps.
→ Cross-app familiarity reduces learning curve to near zero
Hi-fi prototype: edit message, clean delete, calendar invite, direct number text
05 · The Impact

Test results and learnings

4/4
Participants completed all task flows without assistance in in-person usability testing
100%
Of participants had previously used the wrong delete scope: a consistent, fixable error
3
Research methods layered: survey (34) + first-click test (24) + usability testing (4)
✓
What people say is different from what they do: both types of research are necessary
WhatsApp Feature Design
UX Research · Self-initiated · 2021
Back to portfolio
Startup · Logistics · 2017–2018

Same-Day Delivery Platform.
Anything you want,
whenever and wherever.

End-to-end design for an Italian same-day delivery startup: client app, rider app and merchant dashboard. Live and processing real orders within 6 months.

Role
Business Development + UX/UI
Client
Kiwi Five (Italy)
Duration
Dec 2017 – Feb 2018
Status
● Live as of 2021
01 · The Problem

In 2017, same-day delivery in Italy meant restaurants only

Italian consumers were increasingly frustrated with long, unpredictable delivery times — but no service offered same-day delivery for retail products. Small local merchants were losing to Amazon on logistics, despite often having better products and closer proximity to the customer.

Kiwi Five wanted to bring "the shop across the street" online, keeping the feel of a local shop purchase but with same-day, on-demand delivery through a network of independent couriers.

61%
of consumers choose an online store based on delivery times and methods (MetaPack 2016)
47%
of city-dwellers wished shops offered same-day delivery at the time of the project
3
separate interfaces designed: client app, rider app, merchant dashboard
6 mo
from design start to first live orders processed
02 · Research

Three segments, three very different needs

After weeks of stakeholder alignment and interviews with 5 people (colleagues, family, early adopters), I identified three core user segments. Each required a dedicated interface — a unified app would have been too compromised to serve anyone well.

🛍️
Client
Needs something with urgency — a gift, groceries, a forgotten item. Time-poor, smartphone-native. Wants simplicity and speed. Values control: knows what they want and when.
🚴
Rider
Independent contractor. Needs delivery notifications, order details, and a simple accept/decline. The app needs to stay out of their way while they ride. One-handed use, screen time minimised.
🏪
Merchant
Business partnering with Kiwi Five. Needs a simple backoffice to receive and manage orders without disrupting existing operations. Reliability over features.
03 · Design Decisions

Three separate experiences, one platform

01
Separate apps for each segment, not one compromised interface
Rather than a single app trying to serve three very different mental models, we built dedicated experiences: a client app, a rider app, and a merchant dashboard. Each interface was optimised for the specific context and tasks of that segment. No cognitive overhead from irrelevant features.
→ Each user does exactly what they need, nothing more
02
Hybrid iOS/Android app for cost efficiency
Given the startup's constraints, a hybrid build (single codebase for both platforms) was the only viable path to market. This decision was made in collaboration with the development team, balancing time-to-market against technical limitations. No native performance sacrificed for the core use cases.
→ Launched on both platforms within the 6-month window without doubling development cost
03
Paper prototype to real users before any code
We tested paper prototypes and a local software version with real users before committing to development. Iterated on usability issues — many caused by design assumptions — before they became expensive to fix in code. The most valuable session was watching a non-technical user try to complete their first order.
→ Caught critical usability problems early, when changes cost hours rather than weeks
Client-facing app: browse merchants, place orders, track deliveries
Merchant dashboard: incoming order queue and management
05 · The Impact

Launched and processing orders in 6 months

6 mo
MVP launched on iOS and Android within 6 months of design start
3+
Years live after initial launch. Platform still running as of 2021.
3
Separate interfaces designed and delivered: client app, rider app, merchant dashboard
✓
First real experience of end-to-end design at startup speed. Learned more through errors than any course.
Kiwi Five · Same-Day Delivery
Product Design · Startup · 2017–2018
Back to portfolio
Design Challenge · 2021

Contacts & Leads Dashboard.
4 hours, one challenge,
one full-time offer.

A sales lead management dashboard built as a timed design challenge. It led directly to a full-time UX Designer role at Anchor Loans, the leading fix-and-flip lending company in the US.

Role
UX & UI Designer
Duration
4 hours
Tools
Pen & Paper · Adobe XD
Outcome
● Full-time role at Anchor Loans
01 · The Brief

Design a dashboard that helps salespeople manage their leads

The brief: design a web-based dashboard for a sales person to manage their leads, including lead name, lead status (New, Potential, Interested, Has Deal, Submitted Deal), and lead tiers. All technologies must be viable for use today.

I had 4 hours. Before opening any design tool, I spent time understanding the user — drawing on my own experience as a Sales Coordinator using Salesforce daily. Not a day went by without some frustration using Salesforce. This challenge gave me the chance to design the tool I always wished existed.

Dashboard overview: numbers at a glance, team stats, lead status breakdown
02 · Research

Proto-persona from job posts and prior experience

Without access to real users in the 4-hour window, I built a proto-persona using Anchor Loans' public website, job posts, and my own experience in sales coordination. The result was a clear picture of what the user needed and what frustrated them.

🎯
Core needs
Convert prospects into customers. Share projections with management. Speed up loan closings. Access all information at a glance, fast and easy.
😤
Frustrations (from personal experience)
CRM tools requiring too many clicks. Ambiguous status workflows. No quick overview — must dig into individual records. Having to leave the main list to do anything useful.
🧠
Existing patterns they know
Drag-and-drop file management (like a desktop). Click-to-call (like a phone dialer). Filter by category (like any modern list interface). Familiar patterns = zero learning curve.
03 · Design Decisions

Speed and overview above everything

01
Dashboard overview with team stats at a glance
Sales people want to know their numbers without hunting for them. The dashboard gives instant access to pipeline totals, status breakdowns, and team performance — driving both personal accountability and healthy competition. Visible from the moment you open the app.
→ Mirrors the "ambient awareness" experienced sales people rely on to prioritise their day
02
Drag-and-drop status updates from the list view
Rather than opening a modal to change a lead's status, users can drag a lead to the desired status tab in the left navigation. This maps to a pattern every computer user already knows — moving files between folders. Reduces a status update from 4+ clicks to a single drag action.
→ Familiar pattern means zero learning curve
03
Lead detail opens inline, no navigation away from the main list
Clicking a lead opens a detail panel at the bottom of the page, without leaving the list view. From here: edit details, email, call directly, add a note, review offer details, see past activity. No context-switching between pages. The most frequent task shouldn't require page navigation.
→ Keeps the user in their workflow: the biggest complaint about CRM tools
Dashboard: pipeline totals, status breakdown, team performance
Lead detail: inline panel, click-to-call, email, notes, activity history
05 · The Impact

A design challenge that became a career moment

✓
Design challenge led directly to a full-time UX Designer role at Anchor Loans
4 h
Total time including research, proto-persona, sketching, and full Adobe XD mockup
Next
Defined as next step: moderated usability testing with Sales Person and management before developer handoff
✓
Personal experience as a Sales Coordinator using Salesforce daily gave genuine empathy — the best possible research
Contacts & Leads Dashboard
Design Challenge · Anchor Loans · 2021
